channel.title: DutchNews.nl
channel.description: DutchNews.nl brings daily news from The Netherlands in English

item.title: Van Weel in Ghana to rally West African pressure on Bolle Jos
item.description:
Van Weel in Ghana to rally West African pressure on Bolle Jos

July 22, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The Netherlands is trying to build a coalition of West African countries to pressure Sierra Leone into handing over fugitive drugs baron Jos “Bolle Jos” Leijdekkers, justice minister David van Weel said after a visit to Ghana, in an interview with the Telegraaf.

Van Weel told the paper that Sierra Leone’s neighbours, including Liberia and Ghana, are increasingly frustrated at the shelter the country is giving Leijdekkers, whose network operates across the region.

“We are not ruling out any option,” he said, hinting at measures he could not discuss publicly. The Netherlands also wants to set up a permanent secretariat in West Africa to tackle drug smuggling networks, he said.

Sierra Leone’s justice minister, Alpha Sesay, attended the talks on his own initiative and has written to Van Weel proposing to work towards a solution – a sign, according to the Dutch minister, that the pressure is being felt, though he said he saw more willingness to talk than to act.

Leijdekkers, 34, is the Netherlands’ most wanted criminal, sentenced in absentia to prison terms in the Netherlands and Belgium totalling around 80 years for large-scale cocaine smuggling and ordering a murder. He has also been ordered to repay €96 million to the Dutch state.

He is reported to be living under presidential protection in Sierra Leone, where he is said to have a child with the daughter of president Julius Maada Bio. His network has been linked to a record cocaine seizure off the Canary Islands in May, one of the largest single hauls in history.

The regional approach follows a series of failed attempts to get him. Dutch special forces twice came close to arresting him at sea before the operations were called off, and a Dutch push to cut EU development aid to Sierra Leone has so far failed to win support from other member states, ministers told MPs earlier this month.

Prosecutors have meanwhile targeted his family, with his brother extradited from Turkey in June on money laundering charges.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Man arrested after fatal shooting near Vierdaagse festivities
item.description:
Man arrested after fatal shooting near Vierdaagse festivities

July 22, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Police in Nijmegen have arrested a man in connection with a fatal shooting on Tuesday evening, just outside the area where the Vierdaagsefeesten, the street festivities accompanying the annual four-day marches, were taking place.

A young man was shot at around 10pm on the Graafseweg and died later in hospital despite the efforts of ambulance crews and a trauma helicopter team, broadcaster NOS reported. The arrested man is suspected of involvement in the shooting, police said. The circumstances remain unclear.

The city centre was busy at the time because of the marches, which began on Tuesday, and the accompanying festivities. Police told news agency ANP they saw no link between the shooting and the event itself.

The area around the Graafseweg remained cordoned off on Wednesday morning for forensic investigation. Police are looking into whether more people were involved and have appealed for witnesses to come forward.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Police seize servers of porn site Motherless in abuse inquiry
item.description:
Police seize servers of porn site Motherless in abuse inquiry

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Police have raided the hosting company of the pornography website Motherless at locations in Steenbergen, Rotterdam and Amsterdam, seizing servers used by the site, broadcaster NOS has reported.

The public prosecution department is investigating images of possible sexual abuse that appeared on the site, including images of child sexual abuse and of women being assaulted while drugged. Both the people who uploaded the material and the role of the site itself are under investigation, which will be carried out with Europol.

No one has been arrested so far.

Motherless, which had tens of millions of visitors a month, was taken offline by prosecutors in May after reporting by NOS and Nieuwsuur revealed large amounts of illegal material on its servers, hosted by Steenbergen-based provider Nforce. The site later reappeared online via other routes.

The raids are the latest step in a series of Dutch investigations connected to the site. In June, NOS revealed that moderators of the site had themselves posted images of child sexual abuse, with one employee alone uploading 110,000 images.

Earlier, an investigation by CNN had found some 20,000 videos on the site of women being filmed while drugged or asleep, and NRC reported that Dutch users were among those sharing such material.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Class ranking skews school advice for Dutch 12-year-olds: Study
item.description:
Class ranking skews school advice for Dutch 12-year-olds: Study

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The school track advice Dutch children receive at the end of primary school is influenced by how they rank compared with their classmates, even when their test scores are identical, according to research by the Erasmus School of Economics.

Moving up around three places in the class ranking increases a pupil’s chance of a vwo recommendation, the highest track, by about 2.5 percentage points. On the other hand, children near the bottom of their class are more likely to be advised to go to vmbo (the lowest track), regardless of their actual ability.

The advice pupils are given at around age 12 largely determines which type of secondary school they can attend, and the study found that ranking children early on impacts their chances of going to university.

The research suggests teachers use rank as a shortcut because judging absolute ability is hard, while comparing classmates is easy.

The effect was stronger among more experienced teachers, and teachers proved more sensitive to the rankings of children from low-income families and ethnic minority backgrounds, who were more likely to be given lower advice than equally scoring children from better-off homes.

Earlier research has shown that girls and children outside the big cities also tend to receive lower advice than their scores warrant.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Frost in July: Twente records a midsummer first for Netherlands
item.description:
Frost in July: Twente records a midsummer first for Netherlands

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Parts of Twente woke to a touch of ground frost on Tuesday, something never before recorded in the Netherlands at the height of summer.

A temperature of -0.1° was briefly measured at 10 centimetres above the ground at around 5.30am, broadcaster NOS reported. According to weather bureau Weeronline, ground frost had never previously been recorded in the Netherlands between July 20 and August 21.

Since meteorologists began measuring ground temperatures in 1971, ground frost in July has occurred just nine times. The coldest reading, -1.6°, also came in Twente, on July 4, 2019 – a month that went on to produce the opposite extreme, with the hottest temperature ever measured in the Netherlands, 40.7° in Gilze-Rijen, following three weeks later.

Ground frost is slightly more common in June, and in 1955 frost was even recorded at the standard measuring height of 1.5 metres, when Witteveen in Drenthe registered -0.8°.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Dutch ban on Israeli settlement goods to start on September 22
item.description:
Dutch ban on Israeli settlement goods to start on September 22

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The Netherlands will ban the import of goods produced in illegal Israeli settlements from September 22, foreign minister Tom Berendsen has told MPs.

The cabinet announced the trade ban in May but had not yet set a date, broadcaster NOS reported. A majority of MPs called last year for a national ban on the import and transit of goods from illegal settlements in territories occupied by Israel.

The measure covers not only imports but also trading in settlement goods, and Dutch companies operating abroad will have to comply. Activities aimed at getting around the ban are prohibited as well. The measure will apply for three years in principle.

The Council of State raised no objections to the ban in an urgent advisory opinion, although it expressed doubts about how enforceable it will be, a concern the cabinet itself acknowledged when the measure was announced.

The government has repeatedly pushed for an EU-wide ban, but there is insufficient support among member states. Belgium, Spain and Ireland have already introduced sanctions at national level.

Last month, a report by legal organisation Global Echo found that the Netherlands is the top destination for Israeli settlement produce entering the EU market.

The building of settlements in the occupied West Bank and Golan Heights is a violation of international law, and according to the United Nations, settlers are systematically guilty of violence and intimidation against the Palestinian population.

“We want to prevent Dutch society, through its economic activities, from contributing to an unlawful occupation and the maintenance of illegal settlements,” prime minister Rob Jetten said when the ban was agreed in May.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Eindhoven students build world’s first solar-powered ambulance
item.description:
Eindhoven students build world’s first solar-powered ambulance

July 21, 2026 Louis Gore-Langton

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Students at Eindhoven University of Technology have unveiled what they say is the world’s first solar-powered ambulance, designed to bring medical care to remote regions where poor roads and a lack of fuel or electricity put healthcare out of reach.

Stella Juva, presented on Tuesday, was developed in a year by Solar Team Eindhoven, a group of 23 students. Solar panels on the roof power both the motor and the medical equipment on board, and on a sunny day the vehicle is expected to cover 715 kilometres without needing any charging infrastructure.

But how does a solar vehicle fare in one of Europe’s cloudiest countries? “The Dutch weather, to say the least, is not Kenya’s,” team manager Mathijs van Gerven told DutchNews after the launch. The ambulance’s 50 kWh battery stores solar energy so it can keep powering its equipment, and its own motors, without interruption, he explained.

Unlike a traditional ambulance, which transports patients to hospital, Stella Juva is designed to bring care to people, allowing healthcare providers to carry out blood tests, screen for diseases such as tuberculosis and malaria, administer vaccinations and perform pregnancy ultrasounds.

The students developed the vehicle with international health organisations including Amref Health Africa, formerly known as the Flying Doctors. “In remote areas, healthcare workers sometimes travel great distances by bicycle, and reliable energy is not a given,” Femke Maurits of Amref’s Dutch branch said in a statement.

Energy is essential, she said, for keeping vaccines cooled and running medical equipment.

Kenya test
In August the team will take Stella Juva to Kenya, starting with a kick-off at Amref International University attended by officials from the Dutch embassy and Kenyan diplomats. The vehicle will then be tested at two field locations, where healthcare scenarios, such as the treatment of tuberculosis patients, will be simulated.

“Three of our team members have been chosen to drive Stella Juva – they were the first to test her,” Van Gerven told us. “We will see who gets to drive the first Kenyan metres, but I reckon they will all want to go first.”

Afterwards, the ambulance will tour events across the Netherlands, and possibly Europe, to encourage companies and healthcare providers to take the concept further. For production at scale, the team is looking to industry.

“If 23 students are able to do this in just one year, think about what is possible if technological companies and social organisations collaborate,” Van Gerven said.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Lorries ignoring Merwedebrug ban face €500 fines
item.description:
Lorries ignoring Merwedebrug ban face €500 fines

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Roads agency Rijkswaterstaat is installing traffic cameras on the damaged Merwedebrug near Gorinchem to enforce a ban on lorries, with fines of up to €500 for drivers who ignore it.

The bridge on the A27 has been closed since Saturday to freight traffic after an inspection found the bridge’s steel was weaker than expected in several places, and that it could no longer guarantee the structure would hold under heavy traffic.

The roads agency issued an urgent appeal to trucks to avoid using the bridge but many are still driving over it.

An NOS reporter on the scene counted dozens of trucks in half an hour, including vehicles with Dutch license plates.

The new ban will now be enforced via cameras which will automatically read the license plates. Enforcement will be done together with the police and the public prosecution service. Fines can be as high as €500.

Cars, vans, motorbikes, buses and emergency vehicles can still use the Merwedebrug. Freight traffic is being diverted via the A16 and the A2.
item.dc:creator: Molly Quell

item.title: Deaths linked to online medicines prompt national study
item.description:
Deaths linked to online medicines prompt national study

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The addiction research institute Trimbos is starting an investigation into “self-medication”, people buying medicines online without consulting a doctor, it has told broadcaster NOS.

The study has been commissioned by health minister Sophie Hermans, who is worried about injuries and even deaths linked to medicines sold online.

Drugs bought online can be dangerous, according to Laura Smit-Rigter, national coordinator of drug monitoring and policy at Trimbos. “It could involve counterfeit medication,” she told NOS. “In that case, the dosage or composition of the product could be completely different. And that can be life-threatening.”

In 2025, two men who ran a webshop called Funcaps, which sold fake and illegal medicines to the general public, appeared in court in connection with the deaths of at least 27 people.

Funcaps sold a range of substances, among them bromazolam, a sedative, O-DSMT, an opioid painkiller, and 2F-ketamine, an anaesthetic. The products came with no package inserts or usage instructions, and sales were pushed with discount deals and sample packs.

Trimbos wants to find out how many people are buying medicines online and why they turn to the internet instead of going to their doctor.
item.dc:creator: Molly Quell

item.title: Big firms hired 13,500 refugees and asylum seekers last year
item.description:
Big firms hired 13,500 refugees and asylum seekers last year

July 21, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Large companies in the Netherlands hired 13,500 refugees and asylum seekers last year, in an effort to plug staff shortages in sectors such as logistics, hospitality and technical trades.

The figures come from Tent Nederland, a coalition of 51 major employers including Philips, IKEA, ABN Amro, Unilever, Arcadis and Randstad, and were reported by NU.nl. The jobs include both permanent positions and work via staffing agencies.

Tent Nederland was set up in April 2025 as the Dutch arm of the Tent Partnership for Refugees, a global network of large companies founded by Chobani boss Hamdi Ulukaya.

The organisation puts employers in direct contact with refugees, partly through regional job fairs organised with local councils and the refugee settlement agency COA.

Companies are finding it easier to reach refugees, director Hélène van Melle told NU.nl, including asylum seekers, who have been able to work year round since a court scrapped a 24-week annual limit at the end of 2023.

“Employers initially had great difficulty getting in contact with these people,” she said. “But within a year we are seeing that companies are open to taking on asylum seekers.”

Government target
Some firms are recruiting refugees by their own initiative. Construction group Heijmans, which is not a Tent Nederland member, has taken on 60 refugees with residence permits over the past two years.

The hirings fit with government plans to get 75,000 more refugees and promising asylum seekers into paid work by the end of 2030. Work and participation minister Thierry Aartsen wants 60% of new arrivals to have a proper job within five years of being given a residence permit.

Refugee employment has long lagged the rest of the workforce, although CBS figures published in May show new arrivals are finding work faster than a decade ago.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton
